chunk_summary: Maternal vegetarian or vegan diets during pregnancy and lactation can impact infant nutrient stores, requiring proactive supplementation and monitoring to prevent deficiencies, especially vitamin B12 and vitamin D, in the first 1000 days.
learning_objectives: Understand the impact of maternal vegetarian or vegan diets on infant nutrient stores during pregnancy and lactation.
Identify risk factors and screening triggers for maternal and infant vitamin B12 deficiency.
Learn preventive strategies, including supplementation and complementary feeding plans, to avoid infant deficiencies.
Recognize the importance of monitoring infant growth, feeding structure, and neurodevelopmental milestones.
key_facts: Maternal vitamin B12 deficiency can lead to infant symptoms such as poor growth, lethargy, developmental delay, tremors, or neuroregression.
Screening triggers include strict vegan diets, low dairy/egg intake, unexplained macrocytosis, or poor maternal weight gain.
Vitamin B12 supplementation is recommended for vegan mothers and their exclusively breastfed infants.
Routine infant vitamin D prophylaxis is essential during infancy.
Complementary feeding should begin at 6 months with thick cereal–pulse foods and daily iron opportunities.
Omega-3 planning (ALA sources or algae-derived DHA) is advised for highly restrictive diets to support breastmilk fatty acid profiles.
reference_quote: Maternal vitamin B12 deficiency can present in the infant as poor growth, lethargy, developmental delay, tremors, or neuroregression, sometimes before severe anthropometric malnutrition is obvious. (Indian Pediatrics, 2022)
document_key_topics: Maternal vegetarian or vegan diets and infant nutrient stores
Vitamin B12 deficiency prevention in infants
Vitamin D prophylaxis and complementary feeding strategies
